coredata.dc:title: Efficient fabrication of ratiometric fluorescence imprinting sensors based on organic-inorganic composite materials and highly sensitive detection of oxytetracycline in milk
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenjuan
Xu, Yeqing
Liu, Xiqing
Peng, Long
Huang, Ting
Yan, Yongsheng
Li, Chunxiang
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescence imprinting sensors (GQDs/CdTe@MIPs) were successfully constructed by precipitation polymerization method for the recognition and detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) in milk samples. GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), thermal gravity analysis (TGA), UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) techniques. A satisfying linear relationship under 0–80 μM OTC concentration (R2 = 0.99699) was exhibited. High sensitivity was showed with the limit of detection of 3.5 nM and high recovery ranged from 99.93% to 100.20%. In addition, the selectivity of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs to OTC was well comparing with other antibiotics. With the injection of different concentrations of OTC, the fluorescent color change of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs under the 365 nm UV lamp further verified that GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were used successfully for the visual detection to OTC in milk samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxytetracycline
Molecularly imprinted
QDs
Fluorescence
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Specific experimental procedures of CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres
Analysis of OTC content in milk samples
Characterization of GQDs and CdTe QDs
Morphological structures and optical properties of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs
Optimization of the detection conditions of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs
Detection of OTC in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and SEM images of CdTe@SiO2 (a, b) and GQDs/CdTe@MIPs(c, d).
FT-IR spectra of CdTe QDs, CdTe@SiO2 and GQDs/CdTe@MIPs.

Fluorescence spectra and the corresponding linearities of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs (a), GQDs/CdTe@NIPs (b) and GQDs@MIPs (c) under the OTC concentration range of 0–80 μM (inset: corresponding fluorescence color images under the 365 nm UV lamp).
The results for the detection of OTC in milk samples.
Analytical for OTC with different detection methods.

head.ce:title: Efficient fabrication of ratiometric fluorescence imprinting sensors based on organic-inorganic composite materials and highly sensitive detection of oxytetracycline in milk

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials fScience and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials fScience and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials fScience and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric fluorescence imprinting sensors (GQDs/CdTe@MIPs) were successfully constructed by precipitation polymerization method for the recognition and detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) in milk samples. GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), thermal gravity analysis (TGA), UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) techniques. A satisfying linear relationship under 0–80 μM OTC concentration (R2 = 0.99699) was exhibited. High sensitivity was showed with the limit of detection of 3.5 nM and high recovery ranged from 99.93% to 100.20%. In addition, the selectivity of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs to OTC was well comparing with other antibiotics. With the injection of different concentrations of OTC, the fluorescent color change of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs under the 365 nm UV lamp further verified that GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were used successfully for the visual detection to OTC in milk samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC), belongs to one of the most extensively applied tetracycline antibiotics, is a sort of broad-spectrum antibacterial agent and usually used in treatment of bacterial infections [1,2] owing to its low cost and cheap price [3]. However, when using excessive residual of OTC in humans for long term, it will lead to physical discomfort, even cause skeletal deformity and growth inhibition [4]. In addition, OTC may constantly accumulate in biologic chain [5]. Therefore, it is significant to establish highly sensitive detection methods to monitor the content of OTC. Up to now, numerous analysis techniques have been performed for the detection of OTC, including colorimetry [6], enzyme-linked immuno sorbent assay [7,8], thin-layer chromatography [9], electrochemiluminescence [10], high performance liquid chromatography [11–14], fluoroimmunoassay [15] and spectrophometric [16,17] and so on. Among these, fluorescence analysis method is widely applied to OTC detection due to its high sensitivity and rapid response. In recent decades, a large number of relevant studies have been reported. For example, Wang et al. [18] reported highly sensitive fluorometric determination of OTC based on carbon dots and Fe3O4 MNPs. Liu et al. [19] researched the synthesis process of polymers-coated CDs and realized highly selective detection of OTC. In addition, lots of fluorescence detection methods have also been used [1,20]. These literatures further confirm fluorescence analysis own the advantages of rapid and highly selective detection to OTC.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with specific recognition and selective adsorption characteristics, are synthesized by molecular imprinting technique [21–23]. Briefly, template molecules combine with functional monomer by covalent bonds or non-covalent bonds to form pre-polymers [24,25]. Subsequently, pre-polymers form three-dimensional network structures under the action of cross-linking agent. Specific imprinted three-dimensional cavities matching with imprinted molecules are obtained when template molecules are removed by certain methods [26–28]. The fluorescence imprinting sensors comprise molecular imprinting technique and fluorescence analysis, so they not only own high selectivity of molecular imprinting technique, but also fuse the characteristics of simplicity and high sensitivity of fluorescence analysis.

Compared with the single-emission fluorescence sensors, ratiometric fluorescence imprinting sensors are favored by researchers because of its intuitional and reliable detection results. Ratiometric fluorescence imprinting sensors contain two diverse emission wavelengths fluorescence materials. At present, there have been a large of reports about ratiometric fluorescence imprinting imprinting sensors to detect target molecules. For example, Wang et al. has reported a ratiometric fluorescence imprinted assay in order to detect 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid [29]. Liu et al. has constructed ratiometric fluorescence imprinting sensors for visual detection of ciprofloxacin [30]. Ratiometric fluorescence imprinting sensors have been interested tremendously in the field of scientific research due to their visual detection and high detection efficiency.

Recently, composite materials have become a major topic because they are engineering materials consist of several constituent materials with significantly different physical or chemical properties. In composite materials field, polymer-coated inorganic particles emerge well potential applications and excellent properties in the fields of adsorbents, optics and electronics [31]. In this report, we prepared ratiometric fluorescence imprinting sensors (GQDs/CdTe@MIPs) based on organic-inorganic hybrid composite materials. Here, blue graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were response signal and red CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) were reference signal. First of all, silica microspheres capped CdTe QDs were obtained by sol-gel method. Then, the imprinted layers with GQDs were wrapped around the surface of silica by precipitation polymerization at the presence of OTC. GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were obtained after the elution of OTC [32]. Hence, GQDs/CdTe@MIPs possessed corresponding recognition sites of recognizing OTC. By molecular fluorescence spectrophotometer, the fluorescence responses and linear relationship of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs and OTC were measured. The constructed GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were used to OTC visual detection and an obvious fluorescence color changed from blue to pink under the 365 nm UV lamp, which could be observed through naked eyes.

Glutathione (GSH), citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5%), cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O, 99.9%), thioglycollic acid (TGA), 2,2′-Azo-iso-butyronitrile (AIBN), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), oxytetracycline (OTC), trichloroacetic acid, methylacrylic acid (MAA), 3-(methacryloxyl) propyl trimethoxysilane (KH570) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Aladdin reagent Company. (Shanghai, China). Tellurium powder, sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 99%), ammonia solution, NaOH, methanol, ethanol, methylbenzene, acetic acid, penicillin G Na salt (PGS), amikacin (AKC) and azithromycin (ATC) were received from Guoyao Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Kanamycin (KMC) was obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Distilled water was used for solutions preparation and instruments cleaning throughout experiments. All reagents were analytical reagent grade.
The instruments used in the experiment can be found in the support information.

GQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal strategy in a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. Initially, CA and GSH (10:1, w/w) were dispersed into 35 mL distilled water. Then, the mixture was magnetic stirred continuously for 30 min at room temperature to form transparent and uniform solution. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the obtained GQDs were filtered by 0.22 µm filter membranes. The final supernatant was further dialyzed to remove impurities. The product was freeze-dried to collect GQDs powders and dispersed with ultrapure water for following experiments.

The synthesis of CdTe QDs was optimized on the basis of the previously literatures [30,33]. The preparation of precursor solution: 0.4 mM of tellurium powder and 5.3 mM of NaBH4 were added into 2.0 mL distilled water. The mixture was placed in an ultrasonic machine to obtain NaHTe aqueous solution.

Briefly, 365.38 mg CdCl2·2.5 H2O in distilled water was placed into a 250 mL three-necked flask and added 176 μL TGA. Then, the mixture was adjusted pH to 11.2 with NaOH solution (1.0 M). Subsequently, the reaction system was stirred for 30 min and freshly prepared precursor solution was injected quickly under nitrogen atmosphere. Finally, the experimental device was heated to reflux for 7.0 d at 130 °C and red CdTe QDs were obtained. The product was centrifuged with a speed of 8000 rpm for 5.0 min and removed precipitation for following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific experimental procedures of CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 600 μL CdTe QDs were dissolved in 5.0 mL solution containing ethanol and distilled water with the volume ratio of 1:1 in a round-bottomed flask. Meanwhile, 200 μL ammonia solution and 80 μL TEOS were injected into the above solution under continuously magnetic stirring for 10 h at room temperature. At last, the product was centrifuged with a speed of 10000 rpm for 5.0 min and dried in vacuum oven at 60 °C.

To achieve molecularly imprinted layers, double bonds should be firstly modified on the surface of CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres [34]. 100 mg CdTe@SiO2 powder was dispersed in 90 mL methylbenzene. After mixing uniformly, KH570 was added drop by drop and kept stirring at 90 °C for 24 h. CdTe@SiO2-KH570 microspheres were obtained by centrifuging and drying in the vacuum oven overnight.

GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were fabricated by the precipitation polymerization method. In a typical process, 50 mg CdTe@SiO2-KH570 microspheres was dispersed into 60 mL ethanol in a 100 mL one-necked bottle. Subsequently, 400 μL GQDs were added into the above mixed solution and kept constantly magnetic stirring for 30 min. 4.0 mg OTC, 10 μL MAA and 28.1 μL EGDMA were injected the mixture. After stirring for 30 min with nitrogen, 5.0 mg AIBN was put into the solution quickly. The mixture prepolymerized for 6 h at 50 °C and then the whole reaction system stirred constantly for 12 h at 60 °C in the dark. Finally, GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were attained. The products were washed with prepared solvent of methanol/acetic acid (98:2, v/v) to remove templates. In addition, ratiometric fluorescence non-imprinting sensors (GQDs/CdTe@NIPs) were attained through the same procedure in the absence of OTC. As a contrast, single-emission fluorescence imprinting sensors (GQDs@MIPs) were prepared by the same method but without adding CdTe@SiO2-KH570 nanoparticles.

The measurement of fluorescence intensity was scanned via the fluorescence spectrophotometer. Different concentrations (0–80 μM) of OTC solution were prepared. A certain amount of 3.0 g/L GQDs/CdTe@MIPs was placed in a volumetric flask. Meanwhile, the excitation wavelength was 345 nm. The slit widths of excitation and emission were both 10 nm. The photo multiplier tube voltage during the fluorescence detection was set at 900 V. The other correlative measurement was recorded by the fluorescence spectrophotometer using the same procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of OTC content in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk samples were purchased from local supermarket of Jiangsu University in Zhenjiang, Jiangsu. To remove the proteins and fattiness, milk samples was diluted with distilled water and added trichloroacetic acid solution. Afterwards, the solution was centrifuged with a speed of 8000 rpm for 10 min and remove sediment. Subsequently, the mixture was adjusted to neutrality and filtered using 0.22 µm microfiltration membranes. The prepared samples were used for further analysis to validate the accuracy of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs.

The preparation process of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs was showed in Scheme 1 . CdTe QDs were coated by silica layers to obtain CdTe@SiO2 via sol-gel method. The silica layers avoided direct contacting between CdTe QDs and OTC, providing reliable reference signal for ratio detection. In addition, the CdTe@SiO2 shell layers could also effectively decrease the toxicity of CdTe QDs and reduce environmental pollutants. Then, CdTe@SiO2 nanoparticles were modified with KH570 to form double bonds on the surface of SiO2. Afterwards, GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were constructed by the precipitation polymerization method, which using OTC as imprinted templates, GQDs as response signal, MAA as functional monomer, AIBN as evocating agent and EGDMA as cross-linking agent. After solvent extraction and removal of template molecules, a large number of OTC molecular specific recognition sites were remained to selectively identify OTC. Because of rebinding OTC, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs as response signal was rapidly quenched while the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs as reference signal remained almost unchanged. Accordingly, the visual fluorescence color of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs showed distinct change under the 365 nm UV lamp. Therefore, GQDs/CdTe@MIPs successfully realized visualdetection of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the further application of GQDs, the morphology was studied. Fig. S1a showed the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of GQDs, revealing a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation in nanometer scale and the average particle size distribution was approximately 1.25 nm in Fig. S2a. Meanwhile, high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image confirmed that GQDs existed a clear crystal structure with 0.21 nm lattice spacing, corresponding to the (1 0 0) surface of graphite carbon [35]. Furthermore, the TEM image in Fig. S1b showed that CdTe QDs were well-dispersed and the average particle size distribution was approximately 4.5 nm in Fig. S2b.

As shown in Fig. S1c, UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) of GQDs exhibited a strong absorption peak at about 345 nm, regarded as the n-π* transition of the C=O bonds [36]. Under the 365 nm UV lamp, GQDs emitted strong blue photoluminescence. However, it was presented transparent and clear solution in the sunlight. Absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of CdTe QDs in Fig. S1e were observed that the emission wavelength of CdTe QDs lied in 660 nm, while the absorption maximum was at about 630 nm. Meanwhile, under the 365 nm UV lamp, strong red fluorescence color could be observed. Fig. S1d and f displayed the emission spectra of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths and revealed that GQDs owned optimal emission peak when excited at 345 nm. In Fig. S3a, UV–vis spectra of OTC displayed stronger absorption peaks in 278 nm and 362 nm. Fig. S3b revealed excitation fluorescence spectra of OTC with the biggest peak in 390 nm and emission fluorescence spectra of OTC with the strongest emission peak in 532 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological structures and optical properties of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological structures of CdTe@SiO2 and GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were characterized by TEM and scanning electron microscope (SEM) techniques. Fig. 1 a and b displayed the dispersity of CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres was well and the surface of them was very smooth. The average diameter of them was about 250 nm through the size distribution in Fig. S4(a). Nevertheless, after the imprinted layers were decorated, the average diameter of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs changed to about 270 nm in thickness calculated from the TEM image in Fig. 1c and the size distribution was showed in Fig. S4(b). The surface of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs was obviously rougher than that of CdTe@SiO2. So CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres and GQDs/CdTe@MIPs could be easily distinguished in Fig. 1b and d. Consequently, TEM and SEM images exhibited intuitively that GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were synthesized successfully.

Fig. S5 displayed the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs, CdTe QDs and GQDs/CdTe@MIPs. As shown in Fig. S5, GQDs@MIPs (curve a) exhibited a full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of about 70 nm at 435 nm, so the monochromaticity was better. When excited at 345 nm, CdTe QDs (curve b) exhibited an emission peak centering at 660 nm with a FWHM of 60 nm. Under the single excitation of 345 nm, GQDs/CdTe@MIPs (curve c) possessed double emission peaks at 435 nm and 660 nm.

In order to achieve the successful analysis and detection to OTC, template molecules needed first be eluted from GQDs/CdTe@MIPs in Fig. S6. Before the removal of templates, the fluorescence intensity of the response signal section of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs (curve c) was approximately 47% of that of GQDs/CdTe@NIPs (curve a). After the removal of templates, the fluorescence intensity of the response signal section of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs (curve b) recovered to nearly 90% of that of GQDs/CdTe@NIPs. The results indicated that most imprinted template molecules on the surface of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs had been eluted successfully.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in Fig. 2 showed the different vibration peaks of CdTe QDs, CdTe@SiO2 and GQDs/CdTe@MIPs. The distinct absorption peak at 3451 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of -OH. The characteristic peak at 1639 cm−1 was attributed to the C=O bending vibration from carboxyl [37], revealing the presence of TGA on CdTe QDs surface. The peaks at 791 cm−1 and 460 cm−1 revealed Si-O vibration. The broad peak at 1080 cm−1 represented the Si-O-Si bending vibration from TEOS [38]. The absorption peak at 1730 cm−1 was related to C=O stretching vibration [39]. The peaks at 2950 cm−1 (C–H stretching vibration) and 1390 cm−1 (C-H bending vibration) validated the existence of MAA and EGDMA [40]. In general, FT-IR spectra showed that GQDs/CdTe@MIPs were successfully developed.

The composition of CdTe/GQDs@MIPs was investigated by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in Fig. S7. The XPS image in the full range proved the existence of Si 2p (102.7 eV), C 1 s (284.5 eV), N 1 s (399.8 eV) and O 1 s (532.0 eV). The peaks of Si-C (102.88 eV) and Si-O (102.49 eV) in Fig. S8(a) indicated SiO2 was prepared in CdTe/GQDs@MIPs. The narrow scans of C 1s peaks in Fig. S8(b) revealed the signals of C-C at 284.5 eV, C=C at 284.1 eV, C-N/C-O at 285.8 eV and C=O at 288.1 eV [41,42]. The N 1s spectra in Fig. S8(c) implied a broad peak between 396.7 eV and 401.3 eV, which was disintegrated into two peaks. One was the C-N-C bonds signal occurring at 398.5 eV, and the other was the peak at 399.8 eV, which were attributed to the C-NH2 bonds. The O 1s spectra in Fig. S8(d) showed three peaks at 531.9 eV, 531.5 eV, and 532.6 eV, which implied the existence of Si-O, C=O and C-O, respectively [43]. In general, XPS spectra indicated surface-imprinted layer was generated successfully on the SiO2 surface.

Thermal gravity analysis (TGA) experiments were showed in Fig. S9. TGA curves of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs and GQDs/CdTe@NIPs were clearly observed. The whole process of thermal weightlessness process was divided into two parts. When the temperature was about 130 °C, a portion of interior crystallization water and organic solvent of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs and GQDs/CdTe@NIPs were lost in the first part. Furthermore, the weight loss of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs and GQDs/CdTe@NIPs reduced 11.66% and 15.03% at a temperature range of 270–470 °C. The result could be attributed to the decomposition of functional monomer and cross-linking agent. As can be seen from these results, the imprinted layers were graft on the SiO2 surface successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the detection conditions of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: For obtaining better experimental results, we optimized further the detection conditions of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs including fluorescence stability, pH effect and response time. All optimization parameters were performed in the OTC concentration range of 0–80 µM at room temperature.

Additionally, the fluorescence stability of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs was evaluated by testing the fluorescence spectra of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs at room temperature. As exhibited in Fig. S10, the ratio of I435/I660 existed no significant change in 120 min. These results displayed the physical stability of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs was well due to possibly the outstanding protection of imprinting layers.

The pH of test environment played a vital role in fluorescence detection process, because pH effect may change the surface charge of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs. The influence of pH was investigated in details by relative fluorescence intensity (I435/I660)0/(I435/I660) in the interval 3.0–13.0, herein, (I435/I660)0 and (I435/I660) stood for the fluorescence intensity of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs under the conditions of absence and presence of template molecules, respectively. Fig. S11 showed the relative fluorescence intensity of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs was highest when pH was neutral condition. Thus, pH value of 7.0 was selected as optimal condition for the following experiments.

To some extent, the reaction time affected the quenching efficiency of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs, so the reaction time between OTC and GQDs/CdTe@MIPs was studied. In short, the experiment was investigated by adding known concentration of template molecules into GQDs/CdTe@MIPs until the fluorescence intensity of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs hardly changed. As shown in Fig. S12, after the adjunction of template molecules, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs occurred an obvious fluorescence quenching within 10 min. Subsequently, the reaction system reached equilibrium. Therefore, 10 min was determined as the incubation time for further experiments.

Under above standard procedures and optimized conditions, the analytical performances of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs after rebinding different concentrations of OTC were investigated. As can be seen in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity at 435 nm decreased regularly whereas the fluorescence intensity at 660 nm remained almost unchanged with the increase of OTC concentration. Calibration curve was obtained by plotting the fluorescence intensity ratios with different concentration of OTC. A good linearity was found when adding 0, 10 µM, 20 µM, 30 µM, 40 µM, 50 µM, 60 µM, 70 µM and 80 µM OTC. The fluorescence quenching of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs with OTC was satisfied with the following equation: log[(I435/I660)0/(I435/I660)] = 0.00607COTC − 0.00585, and correlation coefficient was 0.99699. In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) was based on 3δ/s, in which δ meant the standard deviation of the blank measurements (replicate detection times n = 13) and s meant the slope of calibration line, and attained down to 3.5 nM. Owing to CdTe QDs as reference signal, the ratios of two emission intensities changed with the decrease of fluorescence intensity at 435 nm. An obvious fluorescence color changed from blue to pink, which could be feasibly observed through naked eyes.

Meanwhile, the dose response of GQDs/CdTe@NIPs to OTC was examined. Fig. 3b showed that the relative fluorescence intensity of GQDs/CdTe@NIPs also reduced under different OTC concentrations. The quenching efficiency was low and correlation coefficient was 0.99069. The fluorescence color change of GQDs/CdTe@NIPs was no obvious to be distinguished. This phenomenon mainly resulted from no corresponding recognition sites in GQDs/CdTe@NIPs to rebind OTC.

For reflecting the advantages of visual detection from GQDs/CdTe@MIPs, the linearity of single-emission GQDs@MIPs were also researched. Similar quenching tendency of GQDs@MIPs was observed with the increase of OTC concentration in Fig. 3c. However, the color variation of fluorescence quenching of GQDs@MIPs was hard to distinguish by naked eyes compared with that of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs. The corresponding linearities of GQDs@MIPs showed correlation coefficient was 0.99085.

Furthermore, the selectivity of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs for OTC was also evaluated by recording log[(I435/I660)0/(I435/I660)] of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs in same conditions with other antibiotics, including PSS, AKC, KMC, ATC. Fig. S13 revealed that the relative fluorescence intensity ratio of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs to OTC was strongly higher than other antibiotics. The inset (a) displayed the fluorescence color of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs transformed to pink when injecting 60 μM OTC. This distinct color variation verified visual detection to OTC compared other antibiotics. As a comparison, the selectivity of GQDs/CdTe@NIPs was also studied. The relative fluorescence intensity ratio of GQDs/CdTe@NIPs was lower than GQDs/CdTe@MIPs. In the meanwhile, the images under the 365 nm UV lamp intuitively illustrated that the fluorescence color had no distinct variance in the inset (b). This might be caused by the presence of a great many corresponding recognition sites in GQDs/CdTe@MIPs and could rebind successfully with template molecules. As a consequence, GQDs/CdTe@MIPs possessed excellent specificity to OTC.

In order to further confirm the practicability of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs, the effect of possible interfering ions was also investigated, including coexisting ions Na+, Ca2+, K+, OH−, Cl−, HCO3 −. Other compounds included lactose, vitamin and OTC. The coexisting ions and compounds had almost no effect to special recognition of OTC by established fluorescence detection method in Fig. S14. The above results indicated that GQDs/CdTe@MIPs owned favorable specific selective recognition toward OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The content of OTC in milk samples is low, which cannot be directly detected. Besides, to realize visual detection to OTC, so we used spiking recovery experiment in real samples. The prepared fluorescence detection method was employed to recognize OTC by adding known concentration of OTC into the milk samples. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries in designed detection system ranged from 99.93% to 100.20%. In the meantime, compared with other detection methods which were summarized in Table 2 , this work proposed fluorescence detection method to OTC displayed a satisfying limit of detection, which validated GQDs/CdTe@MIPs could be applied to analyze milk samples in this work.

In summary, GQDs/CdTe@MIPs, in which GQDs as response signal and CdTe QDs as reference signal, were successfully established based on precipitation polymerization. Owing to the combination of molecular imprinting technique and fluorescence detection, GQDs/CdTe@MIPs showed strong fluorescence stability and satisfying sensitivity for the recognition of OTC. Due to the presence of corresponding recognition sites, the fluorescence color of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs had distinct variance from blue to pink and achieved the visual detection of OTC with the increase of OTC concentration. In addition, this sensitive OTC detection system had better precision and LOD was low to 3.5 nM under the concentration of OTC in the range of 0–80 μM. Moreover, the actual performance of GQDs/CdTe@MIPs was verified in milk samples testing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21607013) and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20160569).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105053.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoparticle enhanced fluorescence of europium (III) for detection of tetracycline in milk
Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H Tan, Y Chen. Silver nanoparticle enhanced fluorescence of europium (III) for detection of tetracycline in milk. Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical. 173 (2012) 262-267.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of citrate in tablets and of oxytetracycline in serum using europium (III) luminescence
Microchemical Journal
